title: Personalised learning strategy for Manoj — full-stack curriculum with toddler, freelancing, and family constraints

sections.title: Your real daily schedule
sections.subtitle: Built around your wife's teaching hours, your toddler's rhythm, and your freelance obligations.

callouts.text: The original plan assumed 2 hrs/day 5 days a week. With a 10-month-old solo from 6am–3pm, that is not your reality. 90 minutes on weekdays is honest and achievable. Don't plan for 2 hours and consistently hit 40 minutes — that kills motivation. Plan for 90 minutes and hit 90 minutes every day.
The 5am early block is the game changer. It requires sleeping by 10pm. This is the single habit adjustment that makes everything else possible. Non-negotiable.

timeBlocks.title: Early morning learning block
timeBlocks.text: Wake before everyone. 30 min: read yesterday's notes, code one small thing. This is your quietest, sharpest time.

timeBlocks.title: Solo baby time
timeBlocks.text: Full attention on the toddler. Feed, play, nap routine. No laptop open — it doesn't work and you'll frustrate yourself.

timeBlocks.title: Freelance work block
timeBlocks.text: Baby's first nap window. This is your income-producing time — protect it for client work.

timeBlocks.title: Baby awake: lunch + play
timeBlocks.text: Full attention again. Use this mentally: replay what you're learning in your head while playing.

timeBlocks.title: Main learning block — 60 min
timeBlocks.text: Baby's afternoon nap. This is your deep work window. Code, build, write — not just read. No distractions.

timeBlocks.title: Review / notes — 30 min
timeBlocks.text: Still in nap window. Write notes on what you just built. Commit code to GitHub.

timeBlocks.title: Wife home — shared baby time
timeBlocks.text: Decompress together. Don't study during this time — your wife needs support too after a full day teaching.

timeBlocks.title: Freelance work block 2
timeBlocks.text: Evening client work, emails, deliverables. Baby usually settles by now.

timeBlocks.title: Evening review — 30 min
timeBlocks.text: After baby is asleep. Re-read notes, watch a short concept video. Never code at night — errors from tired coding waste morning time fixing them.

timeBlocks.title: Deep learning block — 2.5 hrs
timeBlocks.text: Wife is home. This is your uninterrupted time. Build the week's mini-project. This single block is worth 3 weekday sessions.

timeBlocks.title: Family morning time
timeBlocks.text: Breakfast together. Your wife has been working all week — this matters.

timeBlocks.title: Freelance or rest
timeBlocks.text: Client work if deadline-driven, otherwise rest. Don't study in the afternoon — use fresh morning hours only.

sections.askPrompt: My toddler sometimes skips afternoon naps or wakes up early. What should I do on days when my learning window disappears completely?

sections.title: Note-taking system
sections.subtitle: Designed for a busy parent who needs to recall things fast and pick up where they left off.

callouts.text: Don't write notes while learning. First understand, then build, then write notes. Notes written while confused just capture the confusion. Notes written after it clicks capture the insight.

cards.heading: The system you'll use: Notion
items: Use Notion — one workspace called "Fullstack Learning" with a "Concepts" database (table or gallery view)
One page per concept, not one page per day — "JPA Relationships" not "Monday notes"
Add properties to the Concepts database: Phase, Status, and Review date — so you can filter and sort
When you return after baby duty, open your last-edited page and read the last 3 lines — instant context restore
cards.resource: Notion is free for personal use, syncs automatically across desktop and mobile, and works offline in the apps — no separate backup step needed.

cards.heading: Note template — use this for every concept
paragraphs: **What it is:** One sentence in plain English.
**Why it matters:** One sentence on when you'd use it.
**The thing that confused me:** What tripped you up.
**Where I used it:** Which project file and line.

cards.heading: The "resume in 30 seconds" rule
items: Before every break (baby woke up, freelance call, etc.) — write ONE sentence: "I was about to..."
This sentence lets you resume in 30 seconds instead of spending 15 minutes reconstructing context
Keep a sticky note on your monitor with the current task written on it in plain language
Leave your IDE open to the exact file and line you were working on — never close it mid-task

cards.heading: Spaced repetition — review schedule
items: After learning a concept: review after 1 day, then 3 days, then 7 days, then 21 days
In Notion: set the "Review date" property on each Concepts page, then create a filtered/sorted view called "Due for review"
The 9:30pm review block is ONLY for re-reading old concept notes on their review date
Never re-read notes from today at night — that wastes the spaced repetition benefit
cards.resource: Alternative: Anki (free flashcard app) for short definitions like HTTP status codes, JPA annotations, Git commands. 10 cards/day takes 5 minutes and is mobile-friendly for baby-holding moments.

sections.title: How to practice effectively
sections.subtitle: The method that turns reading into real skill — designed for fragmented time blocks.

callouts.text: Struggling for 20 minutes before asking for help is not wasted time — it is the actual learning. The moment you figure it out after that struggle, it sticks permanently. Copy-pasting solutions you don't understand just delays the same confusion to a later session.

steps.title: Watch or read (15 min)
steps.text: Understand the concept at a surface level. Don't take notes yet. Just get a feel for it.

steps.title: Build it yourself from scratch (40 min)
steps.text: Close the tutorial. Recreate what you just saw from memory. Struggle here — that's the learning happening.

steps.title: Break it deliberately (15 min)
steps.text: Remove a line of code. Change a value. Introduce an error. See what happens. Read the error message carefully.

steps.title: Fix it without looking (15 min)
steps.text: Debug from memory. If truly stuck after 10 minutes, then look. But try first.

steps.title: Write your note (5 min)
steps.text: Only now, when you understand it, write the concept note. One short note per session.

cards.heading: The one-session, one-outcome rule
items: Each 60-minute block must produce exactly ONE concrete output — not "I studied JPA"
"I built the Product entity with category relationship and saw the table in MySQL Workbench" — that's a session outcome
Write the outcome in your Obsidian daily note before you start. Forces clarity.
If you can't name the outcome before starting, you're not ready to start — clarify the goal first

cards.heading: Commit after every session — no exceptions
items: Every single session ends with: git add . && git commit -m "what you built" && git push
Even if it's broken — commit with message "WIP: stuck on X"
This creates a timeline of progress and lets you roll back when things go wrong
After 6 months your GitHub contribution graph tells the story of your discipline — that's a portfolio in itself

cards.heading: Mobile learning — use baby time smartly
items: While feeding, rocking, or holding the baby: YouTube on phone — watch Spring Boot / React concept videos (no coding, just absorb)
Amigoscode, Dan Vega, Theo (t3.gg) — short, focused videos that work with one ear on the baby
Anki flashcards on phone — review HTTP status codes, Java annotations, SQL keywords during nap transfer
Read react.dev or spring.io/guides on phone — bookmark pages, read in 5-minute bursts
This passive absorption makes the coding sessions land faster — concepts feel familiar when you sit down to code

sections.title: Staying disciplined over 7–8 months
sections.subtitle: Motivation comes and goes. Systems keep you going when motivation disappears.

callouts.text: The biggest threat to your curriculum is not laziness — it is guilt spiralling. Missing 3 days, feeling behind, trying to cram 6 hours in one day, burning out, stopping for a month. Prevent this: one missed day is just one missed day. Resume normally the next morning.

cards.heading: The weekly planning ritual — Sunday 8pm, 15 minutes
items: Every Sunday evening: open the Phase plan, pick exactly which 5 daily tasks you will complete this week
Write them in a physical notebook — one per day, Mon–Fri. One sentence each.
Don't plan more than 5. Under-planning and over-delivering beats over-planning and failing.
Saturday: review the week — what did you complete? What blocked you? Adjust next week.

cards.heading: The streak system — your toddler-proof habit tracker
items: Track one simple binary metric: "Did I open my code editor today and write at least one line?" — yes or no
Use a physical calendar on the wall. Cross off each day with a red marker. Don't break the chain.
A 5-minute commit on an exhausted day counts. Showing up is the habit — not the output.
Missed days are allowed — but never two in a row. Two in a row becomes a week off.

cards.heading: Phase milestones — know where you are
When to take a full week off — no guilt
items: Baby is sick — full stop. A sick toddler needs 100% of you. Learning can wait 5 days.
Major freelance deadline — protect income first. Learning is the long game.
You haven't slept properly in 4+ days — chronic sleep deprivation makes learning 60% less effective anyway
After any full week off: don't try to catch up. Just resume from where you stopped.

sections.title: Balancing learning with earning
sections.subtitle: You cannot stop earning. Here is how to keep both moving forward.

callouts.text: You already have real project experience — Senevo, Verge3D plugins, WooCommerce configurator. That is a stronger starting point than most people on this curriculum. You are adding structured theory to existing practice, not starting from zero.

cards.heading: The income-first rule
items: Freelance work always wins over learning when there is a deadline or a client waiting
Learning is an investment in future earnings — but right now, current earnings pay for your family
Never miss a client deadline to study. That damages your reputation, which is your livelihood.
When a big project comes in: suspend learning for that sprint, deliver, then resume

cards.heading: Apply learning to real freelance work immediately
items: Month 2 onwards: if a Fiverr client needs a basic Spring Boot API — take it. Build it. You're learning anyway.
Month 4 onwards: if a client needs a React frontend — take it. Your curriculum work IS the deliverable.
This is not dishonest — you are building the skill while delivering. Just scope conservatively.
Each real client project consolidates your learning 10x faster than tutorial exercises

cards.heading: Gradually raise your rates as skills grow
items: After Month 4 (React + Spring Boot deployed): update Fiverr/Upwork — add full-stack to your offering
After Month 6 (tested, dockerised, CI/CD): you can legitimately charge mid-level full-stack rates
Don't wait until you "feel ready" to raise rates — update the profile after each phase completes
Your Senevo experience + this curriculum = a strong story. Frame it as a portfolio, not just skills.

cards.heading: What to avoid taking on during the curriculum
items: Avoid projects that require a technology you haven't reached yet in the curriculum
Avoid scope-creep projects with no clear end date — they consume all your nap-time blocks
Avoid clients who require same-day responses — you can't guarantee that with a toddler
Set your Fiverr response time expectation to 24 hours — be honest about it upfront

sections.askPrompt: How do I update my Fiverr and Upwork profile to reflect full-stack Spring Boot + React skills as I progress through the curriculum — what should I add after each phase?

sections.title: Mindset for the long road
sections.subtitle: What to believe, what to ignore, and how to keep going when it's hard.

callouts.text: 34, freelancer, father of a 10-month-old, learning full-stack development in 90-minute windows while keeping a business running. If you finish this — and you can — it will be one of the most disciplined things you have ever done.

cards.heading: You are doing this in hard mode — acknowledge it
items: Most people learning to code have 4+ free hours a day. You have 90 interrupted minutes.
Most people don't have a 10-month-old who needs them solo for 9 hours a day on weekdays.
Finishing this curriculum in these conditions is genuinely harder than finishing a bootcamp.
Don't compare your pace to someone without a toddler and a freelance income to protect.

cards.heading: Slow progress is still progress
items: 7 months of consistent 90-min sessions beats 3 months of intense study followed by burnout
Your toddler will get more independent every month — by Month 4, your learning windows will expand naturally
Sleep deprivation is real. On days you feel stupid, it's usually sleep, not ability.
Write one sentence in your Obsidian daily note each evening: "Today I learned ___." Read these when you doubt yourself.

cards.heading: Your wife is part of this plan
items: Have an honest conversation: "I'm learning full-stack development. On weekends I need 2.5 hours early morning. I will be fully present the rest of the day."
Her understanding and buy-in makes the 5am sessions possible without resentment building
When you get your first full-stack client project because of this skill — celebrate with her. She made it possible.
If a week is hard for her, pause the early sessions that week. Family first, curriculum second.

cards.heading: The moment it becomes worth it
items: Month 4: you will deploy a full React app live and feel something shift in your confidence
Month 6: a client will ask for something you couldn't do before, and you'll say yes without anxiety
Month 8: your rates will go up and your daughter will be nearly 18 months old — walking, curious, watching you work
The skill you're building is permanent. The toddler phase is temporary. Both are worth it.

sections.askPrompt: I had a week where the baby was sick and I missed all my learning sessions. How do I get back on track without trying to catch up all at once?

sections.title: Templates to follow
sections.subtitle: Reusable templates for Notion and your daily routine — copy these once, then reuse them all the way through the curriculum.

callouts.text: Set these up once in Notion during Week 1 of Phase 1. After that, using them takes seconds and keeps every session consistent.

cards.heading: Notion concept-note template
cards.resource: Create this as a Notion template inside your "Concepts" database so every new page starts pre-filled.
paragraphs: **What it is:** One sentence in plain English.
**Why it matters:** One sentence on when you'd use it.
**The thing that confused me:** What tripped you up.
**Where I used it:** Which project file and line.

cards.heading: Daily "resume in 30 seconds" note
items: Keep one Notion page called "Right now" pinned to your sidebar
Before every interruption, overwrite it with one sentence: "I was about to..."
Include the file name and line number you were editing
First thing when you sit back down: open this page before anything else

cards.heading: Sunday weekly planning template
cards.resource: Use this every Sunday at 8pm — fill it in, then check off each line through the week.
paragraphs: **Saturday review:** What did I complete? What blocked me? What changes next week?

cards.heading: Daily session-outcome template
items: Write this BEFORE you start your main learning block
"By the end of this session I will have: ___" — one concrete, checkable outcome
Bad: "I studied JPA". Good: "I built the Product entity with category relationship and saw the table in MySQL Workbench"
After the session, mark it done or write one line on what blocked you

cards.heading: Commit message template
cards.resource: Run this at the end of every session — even a broken one.
paragraphs: If unfinished: git commit -m "WIP: stuck on <thing> — next: <what to try next>"

cards.heading: "Today I learned" mindset journal
items: One Notion page per month, one line per day
Format: "[date] — Today I learned ___"
Read the whole month back on days you feel stuck or behind
This is your evidence that slow progress is still progress

cards.heading: Freelance profile update template
items: Use this after completing each phase's project (see Phase milestones in Staying disciplined)
Add the phase's mini-project to your portfolio with a one-line description and a GitHub link
Update your Fiverr/Upwork skills list with the technologies from that phase
Re-read "Balancing learning with earning" for when to raise your rates

sections.askPrompt: Help me set up a Notion workspace with a Concepts database, a Right now page, and templates for weekly planning and session outcomes — what's the best structure?
